summary: Luu explores the phenomenon of "normalization of deviance" in software engineering, where dangerous practices become accepted as normal over time despite their risks. Drawing parallels from healthcare, aviation, and other fields, he argues that tech companies routinely normalize practices that would be considered shocking elsewhere—from broken builds to non-existent security to rewarding heroism over prevention. The industry lacks the systematic approach to learning from failures that other fields have developed, instead relying on cargo-culting practices from successful companies without understanding the context.
key_points: Tech companies routinely normalize deviant practices like broken builds, flaky tests marked to pass on any success, and firing people for minor leaks while ignoring major security issues
The normalization of deviance is well-studied in healthcare, aviation, and engineering but rarely discussed in software despite clear parallels in postmortems

Common justifications for breaking rules mirror those in other fields: rules seem inefficient, breaking them seems justified for immediate benefit, people trust themselves, and weak signals from newcomers get ignored
Companies that eventually developed good practices (Google, Microsoft) only did so after being embarrassed by major failures, not through essays or speeches
Strong engineering culture is a much larger productivity multiplier than trendy languages or methodologies, but companies spend far more effort on external metrics than internal processes
money_quotes: The data are clear that humans are really bad at taking the time to do things that are well understood to incontrovertibly reduce the risk of rare but catastrophic events. We will rationalize that taking shortcuts is the right, reasonable thing to do.
new person joins / new person: WTF WTF WTF WTF WTF / old hands: yeah we know we're concerned about it / new person: WTF WTF wTF wtf wtf w... / new person gets used to it

Google didn't go from adding z to the end of names to having the world's best security because someone gave a rousing speech or wrote a convincing essay. They did it after getting embarrassed a few times.

People write a lot about how things like using fancier languages or techniques like TDD or agile will make your teams more productive, but having a strong engineering culture is much larger force multiplier.
It's cargo cults all the way down.
key_insight: Software engineering lacks systematic mechanisms to prevent the normalization of deviance that other fields have developed, relying instead on cargo-culting practices and only learning from catastrophic failures rather than near-misses or chronic dysfunction.
